dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.speech: Fie, fie on all tired jades, on all mad masters, and all foul ways! Was ever man so beaten? was ever man so rayed? was ever man so weary? I am sent before to make a fire, and they are coming after to warm them. Now, were not I a little pot and soon hot, my very lips might freeze to my teeth, my tongue to the roof of my mouth, my heart in my belly, ere I should come by a fire to thaw me: but I, with blowing the fire, shall warm myself; for, considering the weather, a taller man than I will take cold. Holla, ho! Curtis.

dialogue.speaker: CURTIS
dialogue.speech: Who is that calls so coldly?

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.speech: A piece of ice: if thou doubt it, thou mayst slide from my shoulder to my heel with no greater a run but my head and my neck. A fire good Curtis.

dialogue.speaker: CURTIS
dialogue.speech: Is my master and his wife coming, Grumio?

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.speech: O, ay, Curtis, ay: and therefore fire, fire; cast on no water.

dialogue.speaker: CURTIS
dialogue.speech: Is she so hot a shrew as she's reported?

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.speech: She was, good Curtis, before this frost: but, thou knowest, winter tames man, woman and beast; for it hath tamed my old master and my new mistress and myself, fellow Curtis.

dialogue.speaker: CURTIS
dialogue.speech: Away, you three-inch fool! I am no beast.

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.speech: Am I but three inches? why, thy horn is a foot; and so long am I at the least. But wilt thou make a fire, or shall I complain on thee to our mistress, whose hand, she being now at hand, thou shalt soon feel, to thy cold comfort, for being slow in thy hot office?

dialogue.speaker: CURTIS
dialogue.speech: I prithee, good Grumio, tell me, how goes the world?

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.speech: A cold world, Curtis, in every office but thine; and therefore fire: do thy duty, and have thy duty; for my master and mistress are almost frozen to death.

dialogue.speaker: CURTIS
dialogue.speech: There's fire ready; and therefore, good Grumio, the news.

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.speech: Why, 'Jack, boy! ho! boy!' and as much news as will thaw.

dialogue.line: Come, you are so full of cony-catching!

speech.line: Why, therefore fire; for I have caught extreme cold.
Where's the cook? is supper ready, the house trimmed, rushes strewed, cobwebs swept; the serving-men in their new fustian, their white stockings, and every officer his wedding-garment on?
Be the jacks fair within, the jills fair without, the carpets laid, and every thing in order?
All ready; and therefore, I pray thee, news.
First, know, my horse is tired; my master and mistress fallen out.
How?
Out of their saddles into the dirt; and thereby hangs a tale.
Let's ha't, good Grumio.
Lend thine ear.
Here.
There.
This is to feel a tale, not to hear a tale.
And therefore 'tis called a sensible tale: and this cuff was but to knock at your ear, and beseech listening.
Now I begin: Imprimis, we came down a foul hill, my master riding behind my mistress,--
Both of one horse?
What's that to thee?
Why, a horse.

Tell thou the tale: but hadst thou not crossed me, thou shouldst have heard how her horse fell and she under her horse; thou shouldst have heard in how miry a place, how she was bemoiled, how he left her with the horse upon her, how he beat me because her horse stumbled, how she waded through the dirt to pluck him off me, how he swore, how she prayed, that never prayed before, how I cried, how the horses ran away, how her bridle was burst, how I lost my crupper, with many things of worthy memory, which now shall die in oblivion and thou return unexperienced to thy grave.
By this reckoning he is more shrew than she.
Ay; and that thou and the proudest of you all shall find when he comes home. But what talk I of this?

Call forth Nathaniel, Joseph, Nicholas, Philip, Walter, Sugarsop and the rest: let their heads be sleekly combed their blue coats brushed and their garters of an indifferent knit; let them curtsy with their left legs and not presume to touch a hair of my master's horse-tail till they kiss their hands. Are they all ready?
They are.
Call them forth.
Do you hear, ho? you must meet my master to countenance my mistress.
Why, she hath a face of her own.
Who knows not that?
Thou, it seems, that calls for company to countenance her.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Where be these knaves? What, no man at door
To hold my stirrup nor to take my horse!
Where is Nathaniel, Gregory, Philip?

character.name: ALL_SERVING_MEN
character.line: Here, here, sir; here, sir.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Here, sir! here, sir! here, sir! here, sir!
You logger-headed and unpolish'd grooms!
What, no attendance? no regard? no duty?
Where is the foolish knave I sent before?

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: Here, sir; as foolish as I was before.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: You peasant swain! you whoreson malt-horse drudge!
Did I not bid thee meet me in the park,
And bring along these rascal knaves with thee?

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: Nathaniel's coat, sir, was not fully made,
And Gabriel's pumps were all unpink'd i' the heel;
There was no link to colour Peter's hat,
And Walter's dagger was not come from sheathing;
There were none fine but Adam, Ralph, and Gregory;
The rest were ragged, old, and beggarly;
Yet, as they are, here are they come to meet you.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Go, rascals, go, and fetch my supper in.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Where is the life that late I led—
Where are those—Sit down, Kate, and welcome.—
Sound, sound, sound!

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Why, when, I say? Nay, good sweet Kate, be merry.
Off with my boots, you rogues: you villains, when?

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: It was the friar of orders grey,
As he forth walked on his way—
Out, you rogue! you pluck my foot awry:
Take that, and mend the plucking off the other.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Be merry, Kate. Some water, here; what, ho!
Where's my spaniel Troilus? Sirrah, get you hence,
And bid my cousin Ferdinand come hither:
One, Kate, that you must kiss, and be acquainted with.
Where are my slippers? Shall I have some water?

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Come, Kate, and wash, and welcome heartily.
You whoreson villain! will you let it fall?

character.name: KATHARINA
character.line: Patience, I pray you; 'twas a fault unwilling.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: A whoreson beetle-headed, flap-ear'd knave!
Come, Kate, sit down; I know you have a stomach.
Will you give thanks, sweet Kate; or else shall I?
What's this? mutton?

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: 'Tis burnt; and so is all the meat.
What dogs are these! Where is the rascal cook?
How durst you, villains, bring it from the dresser,
And serve it thus to me that love it not?
There, take it to you, trenchers, cups, and all;
character.action: Throws the meat about the stage
character.line: You heedless joltheads and unmanner'd slaves!
What, do you grumble? I'll be with you straight.

character.name: KATHARINA
character.line: I pray you, husband, be not so disquiet:
The meat was well, if you were so contented.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: I tell thee, Kate, 'twas burnt and dried away;
And I expressly am forbid to touch it,
For it engenders choler, planteth anger;
And better 'twere that both of us did fast,
Since, of ourselves, ourselves are choleric,
Than feed it with such over-roasted flesh.
Be patient; to-morrow 't shall be mended,
And, for this night, we'll fast for company:
Come, I will bring thee to thy bridal chamber.

character.name: NATHANIEL
character.line: Peter, didst ever see the like?

character.name: PETER
character.line: He kills her in her own humour.

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: Where is he?

character.name: CURTIS
character.line: In her chamber, making a sermon of continency to her;
And rails, and swears, and rates, that she, poor soul,
Knows not which way to stand, to look, to speak,
And sits as one new-risen from a dream.
Away, away! for he is coming hither.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Thus have I politicly begun my reign,
And 'tis my hope to end successfully.
My falcon now is sharp and passing empty;
And till she stoop she must not be full-gorged,
For then she never looks upon her lure.
Another way I have to man my haggard,
To make her come and know her keeper's call,
That is, to watch her, as we watch these kites
That bate and beat and will not be obedient.
She eat no meat to-day, nor none shall eat;
Last night she slept not, nor to-night she shall not;
As with the meat, some undeserved fault
I'll find about the making of the bed;
And here I'll fling the pillow, there the bolster,
This way the coverlet, another way the sheets:
Ay, and amid this hurly I intend
That all is done in reverend care of her;
And in conclusion she shall watch all night:
And if she chance to nod I'll rail and brawl
And with the clamour keep her still awake.
This is a way to kill a wife with kindness;
And thus I'll curb her mad and headstrong humour.
He that knows better how to tame a shrew,
Now let him speak: 'tis charity to show.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Is't possible, friend Licio, that Mistress Bianca
Doth fancy any other but Lucentio?
I tell you, sir, she bears me fair in hand.

dialogue.speaker: Hortensio
dialogue.line: Sir, to satisfy you in what I have said,
Stand by and mark the manner of his teaching.

dialogue.speaker: Lucentio
dialogue.line: Now, mistress, profit you in what you read?

dialogue.speaker: Bianca
dialogue.line: What, master, read you? first resolve me that.

dialogue.speaker: Lucentio
dialogue.line: I read that I profess, the Art to Love.

dialogue.speaker: Bianca
dialogue.line: And may you prove, sir, master of your art!

dialogue.speaker: Lucentio
dialogue.line: While you, sweet dear, prove mistress of my heart!

dialogue.speaker: Hortensio
dialogue.line: Quick proceeders, marry! Now, tell me, I pray,
You that durst swear at your mistress Bianca
Loved none in the world so well as Lucentio.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: O despiteful love! unconstant womankind!
I tell thee, Licio, this is wonderful.

dialogue.speaker: Hortensio
dialogue.line: Mistake no more: I am not Licio,
Nor a musician, as I seem to be;
But one that scorn to live in this disguise,
For such a one as leaves a gentleman,
And makes a god of such a cullion:
Know, sir, that I am call'd Hortensio.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Signior Hortensio, I have often heard
Of your entire affection to Bianca,
And since mine eyes are witness of her lightness,
I will with you, if you be so contented,
Forswear Bianca and her love for ever.

dialogue.speaker: Hortensio
dialogue.line: See, how they kiss and court! Signior Lucentio,
Here is my hand, and here I firmly vow
Never to woo her no more, but do forswear her,
As one unworthy all the former favours
That I have fondly flatter'd her withal.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: And here I take the unfeigned oath,
Never to marry with her though she would entreat:
Fie on her! see, how beastly she doth court him!

dialogue.speaker: Hortensio
dialogue.line: Would all the world but he had quite forsworn!
For me, that I may surely keep mine oath,
I will be married to a wealthy widow,
Ere three days pass, which hath as long loved me
As I have loved this proud disdainful haggard.
And so farewell, Signior Lucentio.
Kindness in women, not their beauteous looks,
Shall win my love: and so I take my leave,
In resolution as I swore before.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Mistress Bianca, bless you with such grace
As 'longeth to a lover's blessed case!
Nay, I have ta'en you napping, gentle love,
And have forsworn you with Hortensio.

dialogue.speaker: Bianca
dialogue.line: Tranio, you jest: but have you both forsworn me?

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Mistress, we have.

dialogue.speaker: Lucentio
dialogue.line: Then we are rid of Licio.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: I' faith, he'll have a lusty widow now,
That shall be woo'd and wedded in a day.

dialogue.speaker: Bianca
dialogue.line: God give him joy!

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Ay, and he'll tame her.

character.name: BIANCA
character.line: He says so, Tranio.

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: Faith, he is gone unto the taming-school.

character.name: BIANCA
character.line: The taming-school! what, is there such a place?

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: Ay, mistress, and Petruchio is the master;
That teacheth tricks eleven and twenty long,
To tame a shrew and charm her chattering tongue.

character.name: BIONDELLO
character.line: O master, master, I have watch'd so long
That I am dog-weary; but at last I spied
An ancient angel coming down the hill,
Will serve the turn.

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: What is he, Biondello?

character.name: BIONDELLO
character.line: Master, a mercatante, or a pedant,
I know not what; but formal in apparel,
In gait and countenance surely like a father.

character.name: LUCENTIO
character.line: And what of him, Tranio?

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: If he be credulous and trust my tale,
I'll make him glad to seem Vincentio,
And give assurance to Baptista Minola,
As if he were the right Vincentio.
Take in your love, and then let me alone.

character.name: PEDANT
character.line: God save you, sir!

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: And you, sir! you are welcome.
Travel you far on, or are you at the farthest?

character.name: PEDANT
character.line: Sir, at the farthest for a week or two;
But then up further, and as far as Rome;
And so to Tripoli, if God lend me life.

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: What countryman, I pray?

character.name: PEDANT
character.line: Of Mantua.

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: Of Mantua, sir? marry, God forbid!
And come to Padua, careless of your life?

character.name: PEDANT
character.line: My life, sir! how, I pray? for that goes hard.

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: 'Tis death for any one in Mantua
To come to Padua. Know you not the cause?
Your ships are stay'd at Venice, and the duke,
For private quarrel 'twixt your duke and him,
Hath publish'd and proclaim'd it openly:
'Tis marvel, but that you are but newly come,
You might have heard it else proclaim'd about.

character.name: PEDANT
character.line: Alas! sir, it is worse for me than so;
For I have bills for money by exchange
From Florence and must here deliver them.

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: Well, sir, to do you courtesy,
This will I do, and this I will advise you:
First, tell me, have you ever been at Pisa?

character.name: PEDANT
character.line: Ay, sir, in Pisa have I often been,
Pisa renowned for grave citizens.

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: Among them know you one Vincentio?

character.name: PEDANT
character.line: I know him not, but I have heard of him,
A merchant of incomparable wealth.

character.name: TRANIO
character.line: He is my father, sir; and, sooth to say,
In countenance somewhat doth resemble you.

play.aside: [Aside] As much as an apple doth an oyster, and all one.

dialogue.speaker: TRANIO
dialogue.line: To save your life in this extremity,
This favour will I do for his sake;
And think it not the worst of any your fortunes
That you are like to Sir Vincentio.
His name and credit shall you undertake,
And in my house you shall be friendly lodged:
Look that you take upon you as you should;
You understand me, sir: so shall you stay
Till you have done your business in the city:
If this be courtesy, sir, accept of it.

dialogue.speaker: Pedant
dialogue.line: O sir, I do; and will repute you ever
The patron of my life and liberty.

dialogue.speaker: TRANIO
dialogue.line: Then go with me to make the matter good.
This, by the way, I let you understand;
my father is here look'd for every day,
To pass assurance of a dower in marriage
'Twixt me and one Baptista's daughter here:
In all these circumstances I'll instruct you:
Go with me to clothe you as becomes you.

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.line: No, no, forsooth; I dare not for my life.

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: The more my wrong, the more his spite appears:
What, did he marry me to famish me?
Beggars, that come unto my father's door,
Upon entreaty have a present alms;
If not, elsewhere they meet with charity:
But I, who never knew how to entreat,
Nor never needed that I should entreat,
Am starved for meat, giddy for lack of sleep,
With oaths kept waking and with brawling fed:
And that which spites me more than all these wants,
He does it under name of perfect love;
As who should say, if I should sleep or eat,
'Twere deadly sickness or else present death.
I prithee go and get me some repast;
I care not what, so it be wholesome food.

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.line: What say you to a neat's foot?

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: 'Tis passing good: I prithee let me have it.

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.line: I fear it is too choleric a meat.
How say you to a fat tripe finely broil'd?

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: I like it well: good Grumio, fetch it me.

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.line: I cannot tell; I fear 'tis choleric.
What say you to a piece of beef and mustard?

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: A dish that I do love to feed upon.

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.line: Ay, but the mustard is too hot a little.

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: Why then, the beef, and let the mustard rest.

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.line: Nay then, I will not: you shall have the mustard,
Or else you get no beef of Grumio.

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: Then both, or one, or anything thou wilt.

dialogue.speaker: GRUMIO
dialogue.line: Why then, the mustard without the beef.

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: Go, get thee gone, thou false deluding slave,
That feed'st me with the very name of meat:
Sorrow on thee and all the pack of you,
That triumph thus upon my misery!
Go, get thee gone, I say.

scene.stage_direction: Enter PETRUCHIO and HORTENSIO with meat

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: How fares my Kate? What, sweeting, all amort?

dialogue.speaker: HORTENSIO
dialogue.line: Mistress, what cheer?

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: Faith, as cold as can be.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: Pluck up thy spirits; look cheerfully upon me.
Here love; thou see'st how diligent I am
To dress thy meat myself and bring it thee:
I am sure, sweet Kate, this kindness merits thanks.
What, not a word? Nay, then thou lovest it not;
And all my pains is sorted to no proof.
Here, take away this dish.

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: I pray you, let it stand.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: The poorest service is repaid with thanks;
And so shall mine, before you touch the meat.

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: I thank you, sir.

dialogue.speaker: HORTENSIO
dialogue.line: Signior Petruchio, fie! you are to blame.
Come, mistress Kate, I'll bear you company.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO

aside.line: Eat it up all, Hortensio, if thou lovest me.
Much good do it unto thy gentle heart!

dialogue.line: Kate, eat apace: and now, my honey love,
Will we return unto thy father's house
And revel it as bravely as the best,
With silken coats and caps and golden rings,
With ruffs and cuffs and farthingales and things;
With scarfs and fans and double change of bravery,
With amber bracelets, beads and all this knavery.
What, hast thou dined? The tailor stays thy leisure,
To deck thy body with his ruffling treasure.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: Come, tailor, let us see these ornaments;
Lay forth the gown.

dialogue.speaker: HABERDASHER
dialogue.line: Here is the cap your worship did bespeak.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: Why, this was moulded on a porringer;
A velvet dish: fie, fie! 'tis lewd and filthy;
Why, 'tis a cockle or a walnut-shell,
A knack, a toy, a trick, a baby's cap:
Away with it! come, let me have a bigger.

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: I'll have no bigger: this doth fit the time,
And gentlewomen wear such caps as these.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: When you are gentle, you shall have one too,
And not till then.

aside.line: That will not be in haste.

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: Why, sir, I trust I may have leave to speak;
And speak I will; I am no child, no babe:
Your betters have endured me say my mind,
And if you cannot, best you stop your ears.
My tongue will tell the anger of my heart,
Or else my heart concealing it will break;
And rather than it shall, I will be free
Even to the uttermost, as I please, in words.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: Why, thou say'st true; it is a paltry cap,
A custard-coffin, a bauble, a silken pie:
I love thee well, in that thou likest it not.

dialogue.speaker: KATHARINA
dialogue.line: Love me or love me not, I like the cap;
And it I will have, or I will have none.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: Thy gown? why, ay: come, tailor, let us see't.
O mercy, God! what masquing stuff is here?
What's this? a sleeve? 'tis like a demi-cannon:
What, up and down, carved like an apple-tart?
Here's snip and nip and cut and slish and slash,
Like to a censer in a barber's shop:
Why, what, i' devil's name, tailor, call'st thou this?

character.name: HORTENSIO
character.aside: I see she's like to have neither cap nor gown.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: You bid me make it orderly and well,
According to the fashion and the time.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Marry, and did; but if you be remember'd,
I did not bid you mar it to the time.
Go, hop me over every kennel home,
For you shall hop without my custom, sir:
I'll none of it: hence! make your best of it.

character.name: KATHARINA
character.line: I never saw a better-fashion'd gown,
More quaint, more pleasing, nor more commendable:
Belike you mean to make a puppet of me.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Why, true; he means to make a puppet of thee.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: She says your worship means to make a puppet of her.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: O monstrous arrogance! Thou liest, thou thread,
thou thimble,
Thou yard, three-quarters, half-yard, quarter, nail!
Thou flea, thou nit, thou winter-cricket thou!
Braved in mine own house with a skein of thread?
Away, thou rag, thou quantity, thou remnant;
Or I shall so be-mete thee with thy yard

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: As thou shalt think on prating whilst thou livest!
I tell thee, I, that thou hast marr'd her gown.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: Your worship is deceived; the gown is made
Just as my master had direction:
Grumio gave order how it should be done.

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: I gave him no order; I gave him the stuff.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: But how did you desire it should be made?

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: Marry, sir, with needle and thread.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: But did you not request to have it cut?

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: Thou hast faced many things.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: I have.

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: Face not me: thou hast braved many men; brave not me;
I will neither be faced nor braved. I say unto thee,
I bid thy master cut out the gown; but I did not bid him cut it to pieces: ergo, thou liest.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: Why, here is the note of the fashion to testify.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Read it.

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: The note lies in's throat, if he say I said so.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: Imprimis, a loose-bodied gown:

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: Master, if ever I said loose-bodied gown, sew me in the skirts of it, and beat me to death with a bottom of brown thread: I said a gown.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Proceed.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: With a small compassed cape:

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: I confess the cape.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: With a trunk sleeve:

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: I confess two sleeves.

character.name: Tailor
character.line: The sleeves curiously cut.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Ay, there's the villainy.

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: Error i' the bill, sir; error i' the bill.
I commanded the sleeves should be cut out and sewed up again; and that I'll prove upon thee, though thy little finger be armed in a thimble.

character.name: TAILOR
character.line: This is true that I say: an I had thee in place where, thou shouldst know it.

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: I am for thee straight: take thou the bill, give me thy mete-yard, and spare not me.

character.name: HORTENSIO
character.line: God-a-mercy, Grumio! then he shall have no odds.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Well, sir, in brief, the gown is not for me.

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: You are i' the right, sir: 'tis for my mistress.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Go, take it up unto thy master's use.

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: Villain, not for thy life: take up my mistress' gown for thy master's use!

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Why, sir, what's your conceit in that?

character.name: GRUMIO
character.line: O, sir, the conceit is deeper than you think for:
Take up my mistress' gown to his master's use!
O, fie, fie, fie!

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Hortensio, say thou wilt see the tailor paid.
Go take it hence; be gone, and say no more.

character.name: HORTENSIO
character.line: Tailor, I'll pay thee for thy gown tomorrow:
Take no unkindness of his hasty words:
Away! I say; commend me to thy master.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: Well, come, my Kate; we will unto your father's
Even in these honest mean habiliments:
Our purses shall be proud, our garments poor;
For 'tis the mind that makes the body rich;
And as the sun breaks through the darkest clouds,
So honour peereth in the meanest habit.
What is the jay more precious than the lark,
Because his feathers are more beautiful?
Or is the adder better than the eel,
Because his painted skin contents the eye?
O, no, good Kate; neither art thou the worse
For this poor furniture and mean array.
If thou account'st it shame, lay it on me;
And therefore frolic: we will hence forthwith,
To feast and sport us at thy father's house.
Go, call my men, and let us straight to him;
And bring our horses unto Long-lane end;
There will we mount, and thither walk on foot.
Let's see; I think 'tis now some seven o'clock,
And well we may come there by dinner-time.

character.name: KATHARINA
character.line: I dare assure you, sir, 'tis almost two;
And 'twill be supper-time ere you come there.

character.name: PETRUCHIO
character.line: It shall be seven ere I go to horse:
Look, what I speak, or do, or think to do,
You are still crossing it. Sirs, let't alone:
I will not go to-day; and ere I do,
It shall be what o'clock I say it is.

character.name: HORTENSIO
character.line: Why, so this gallant will command the sun.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Sir, this is the house: please it you that I call?

dialogue.speaker: Pendant
dialogue.line: Ay, what else? and but I be deceived
Signior Baptista may remember me,
Near twenty years ago, in Genoa,
Where we were lodgers at the Pegasus.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: 'Tis well; and hold your own, in any case,
With such austerity as 'longeth to a father.

dialogue.speaker: Pendant
dialogue.line: I warrant you.

dialogue.speaker: Biondello
dialogue.line: But, sir, here comes your boy;
'Twere good he were school'd.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Fear you not him. Sirrah Biondello,
Now do your duty thoroughly, I advise you:
Imagine 'twere the right Vincentio.

dialogue.speaker: Biondello
dialogue.line: Tut, fear not me.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: But hast thou done thy errand to Baptista?

dialogue.speaker: Biondello
dialogue.line: I told him that your father was at Venice,
And that you look'd for him this day in Padua.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Thou'rt a tall fellow: hold thee that to drink.
Here comes Baptista: set your countenance, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Signior Baptista, you are happily met.
Sir, this is the gentleman I told you of:
I pray you stand good father to me now,
Give me Bianca for my patrimony.

dialogue.speaker: Pendant
dialogue.line: Soft son!
Sir, by your leave: having come to Padua
To gather in some debts, my son Lucentio
Made me acquainted with a weighty cause
Of love between your daughter and himself:
And, for the good report I hear of you
And for the love he beareth to your daughter
And she to him, to stay him not too long,
I am content, in a good father's care,
To have him match'd; and if you please to like
No worse than I, upon some agreement
Me shall you find ready and willing
With one consent to have her so bestow'd;
For curious I cannot be with you,
Signior Baptista, of whom I hear so well.

dialogue.speaker: Baptista
dialogue.line: Sir, pardon me in what I have to say:
Your plainness and your shortness please me well.
Right true it is, your son Lucentio here
Doth love my daughter and she loveth him,
Or both dissemble deeply their affections:
And therefore, if you say no more than this,
That like a father you will deal with him,
And pass my daughter a sufficient dower,
The match is made, and all is done:
Your son shall have my daughter with consent.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: I thank you, sir. Where then do you know best
We be affied and such assurance ta'en
As shall with either part's agreement stand?

dialogue.speaker: Baptista
dialogue.line: Not in my house, Lucentio; for, you know,
Pitchers have ears, and I have many servants:
Besides, old Gremio is hearkening still;
And happily we might be interrupted.

dialogue.speaker: Tranio
dialogue.line: Then at my lodging, an it like you:
There doth my father lie; and there, this night,
We'll pass the business privately and well.
Send for your daughter by your servant here:
My boy shall fetch the scrivener presently.
The worst is this, that, at so slender warning,
You are like to have a thin and slender pittance.

dialogue.speaker: Baptista
dialogue.line: It likes me well. Biondello, hie you home,
And bid Bianca make her ready straight;
And, if you will, tell what hath happened.

line.speaker: NARRATION
line.text: Lucentio's father is arrived in Padua, And how she's like to be Lucentio's wife.

line.speaker: BIONDELLO
line.text: I pray the gods she may with all my heart!

line.speaker: TRANIO
line.text: Dally not with the gods, but get thee gone.

line.speaker: TRANIO
line.text: Signior Baptista, shall I lead the way? Welcome! one mess is like to be your cheer: Come, sir; we will better it in Pisa.

line.speaker: BAPTISTA
line.text: I follow you.

line.speaker: BIONDELLO
line.text: Cambio!

line.speaker: LUCENTIO
line.text: What sayest thou, Biondello?

line.speaker: BIONDELLO
line.text: You saw my master wink and laugh upon you?

line.speaker: LUCENTIO
line.text: Biondello, what of that?

line.speaker: BIONDELLO
line.text: Faith, nothing; but has left me here behind, to expound the meaning or moral of his signs and tokens.

line.speaker: LUCENTIO
line.text: I pray thee, moralize them.

line.speaker: BIONDELLO
line.text: Then thus. Baptista is safe, talking with the deceiving father of a deceitful son.

line.speaker: LUCENTIO
line.text: And what of him?

line.speaker: BIONDELLO
line.text: His daughter is to be brought by you to the supper.

line.speaker: LUCENTIO
line.text: And then?

line.speaker: BIONDELLO
line.text: The old priest of Saint Luke's church is at your command at all hours.

line.speaker: LUCENTIO
line.text: And what of all this?

line.speaker: BIONDELLO
line.text: I cannot tell; expect they are busied about a counterfeit assurance: take you assurance of her, 'cum privilegio ad imprimendum solum;' to the church; take the priest, clerk, and some sufficient honest witnesses: If this be not that you look for, I have no more to say, But bid Bianca farewell for ever and a day.

line.speaker: LUCENTIO
line.text: Hearest thou, Biondello?

line.speaker: BIONDELLO
LUCENTIO
line.text: I may, and will, if she be so contented: She will be pleased; then wherefore should I doubt? Hap what hap may, I'll roundly go about her: It shall go hard if Cambio go without her.

speech.speaker: PETRUCHIO
speech.line: Come on, i' God's name; once more toward our father's.
Good Lord, how bright and goodly shines the moon!

speech.speaker: KATHARINA
speech.line: The moon! the sun: it is not moonlight now.

speech.speaker: PETRUCHIO
speech.line: I say it is the moon that shines so bright.

speech.speaker: KATHARINA
speech.line: I know it is the sun that shines so bright.

speech.speaker: PETRUCHIO
speech.line: Now, by my mother's son, and that's myself,
It shall be moon, or star, or what I list,
Or ere I journey to your father's house.
Go on, and fetch our horses back again.
Evermore cross'd and cross'd; nothing but cross'd!

speech.speaker: HORTENSIO
speech.line: Say as he says, or we shall never go.

speech.speaker: KATHARINA
speech.line: Forward, I pray, since we have come so far,
And be it moon, or sun, or what you please:
An if you please to call it a rush-candle,
Henceforth I vow it shall be so for me.

speech.speaker: PETRUCHIO
speech.line: I say it is the moon.

speech.speaker: KATHARINA
speech.line: I know it is the moon.

speech.speaker: PETRUCHIO
speech.line: Nay, then you lie: it is the blessed sun.

speech.speaker: KATHARINA
speech.line: Then, God be bless'd, it is the blessed sun:
But sun it is not, when you say it is not;
And the moon changes even as your mind.
What you will have it named, even that it is;
And so it shall be so for Katharina.

speech.speaker: HORTENSIO
speech.line: Petruchio, go thy ways; the field is won.

speech.speaker: PETRUCHIO
speech.line: Well, forward, forward! thus the bowl should run,
And not unluckily against the bias.
But, soft! company is coming here.

speech.speaker: PETRUCHIO
speech.line: Good morrow, gentle mistress: where away?
Tell me, sweet Kate, and tell me truly too,
Hast thou beheld a fresher gentlewoman?
Such war of white and red within her cheeks!
What stars do spangle heaven with such beauty,
As those two eyes become that heavenly face?
Fair lovely maid, once more good day to thee.
Sweet Kate, embrace her for her beauty's sake.

speech.speaker: HORTENSIO
speech.line: A will make the man mad, to make a woman of him.

speech.speaker: KATHARINA
speech.line: Young budding virgin, fair and fresh and sweet,
Whither away, or where is thy abode?
Happy the parents of so fair a child;
Happier the man, whom favourable stars
Allot thee for his lovely bed-fellow!

speech.speaker: PETRUCHIO
speech.line: Why, how now, Kate! I hope thou art not mad:
This is a man, old, wrinkled, faded, wither'd,
And not a maiden, as thou say'st he is.

speech.speaker: KATHARINA
speech.line: Pardon, old father, my mistaking eyes,
That have been so bedazzled with the sun
That everything I look on seemeth green:
Now I perceive thou art a reverend father;
Pardon, I pray thee, for my mad mistaking.

speech.speaker: PETRUCHIO
speech.line: Do, good old grandsire; and withal make known
Which way thou travellest: if along with us,
We shall be joyful of thy company.

speech.speaker: VINCENTIO
speech.line: Fair sir, and you my merry mistress,
That with your strange encounter much amazed me,
My name is call'd Vincentio; my dwelling Pisa;
And bound I am to Padua; there to visit
A son of mine, which long I have not seen.

dialogue.speaker: VINCENTIO
dialogue.line: Lucentio, gentle sir.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: Happily we met; the happier for thy son.
And now by law, as well as reverend age,
I may entitle thee my loving father:
The sister to my wife, this gentlewoman,
Thy son by this hath married. Wonder not,
Nor be grieved: she is of good esteem,
Her dowery wealthy, and of worthy birth;
Beside, so qualified as may beseem
The spouse of any noble gentleman.
Let me embrace with old Vincentio,
And wander we to see thy honest son,
Who will of thy arrival be full joyous.

dialogue.speaker: VINCENTIO
dialogue.line: But is it true? or else is it your pleasure,
Like pleasant travellers, to break a jest
Upon the company you overtake?

dialogue.speaker: HORTENSIO
dialogue.line: I do assure thee, father, so it is.

dialogue.speaker: PETRUCHIO
dialogue.line: Come, go along, and see the truth hereof;
For our first merriment hath made thee jealous.

dialogue.speaker: HORTENSIO
dialogue.line: Well, Petruchio, this has put me in heart.
Have to my widow! and if she be froward,
Then hast thou taught Hortensio to be untoward.
